responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Defensive Reactions to Infantile Separation Anxiety

Katherine Tennes and E. Lampl Ph.D.

E. Lampl

Katherine H. Tennes, M.A.
E. Esther Lampl, Ph.D.
University of Colorado School of Medicine Department of Psychiatry
4200 East 9th Avenue
Denver, Colo. 80220

E. Lampl  

SUMMARY

Anxiety reactions to experimentally induced minor separations from mother were observed in a group of normal infants from seven months to three years of age.

Behaviors which resulted in reduction of the manifestations of anxiety were categorized into four roughly successive stages:

1. A physiological motor regression combined with avoidance of visual perceptions as a kinesthetic means of restituting the missing mother;

2. Attempts at active mastery of the environment through efforts to follow the mother and overcome intervening obstacles;

3. Inhibition of activity as a defense against anxiety aroused at failure of attempts at active mastery;

4. Use of inanimate objects to implement the relinquishment of infantile separation anxiety—in girls, "giving" representing an identification with the mother, and in boys, "possession" and

"hitting" representing displacements of the object of the libidinal and aggressive drives.

These behaviors constitute a very partial list of possible responses to anxiety in the transitional period between early sensorimotor behaviors and later defense mechanisms.

[[RunningHead]]

Organismic Equilibration and the Energy-Structure Duality in Psychoanalytic Theory—An Attempt at Theoretical Refinement

Riley Gardner Ph.D.

Riley Gardner

Riley W. Gardner, Ph.D.
The Menninger Foundation
Topeka, Kansas, 69601

Riley Gardner  

SUMMARY

As Freud so cogently indicated, abstractions employed in early stages of theory formation are often inadequate, although useful, and should later be replaced by more effective explanatory principles. Two common characteristics of preliminary abstractions are their necessary redundancy and their excessive closeness to the phenomena they are intended to explain. As a result, preliminary abstractions concerning fields of forces, including the field of forces that is the human organism, usually involve redundant

36

abstractions whose interrelationships are conceived in terms of force and counterforce at the level of pairs or groups of component forces or structures. More adequate and parsimonious concepts of such fields of forces require that the theoretician view each part process from the vantage point of the totality, rather than attempt to construct a totality by postulating more and more elaborate combinations of low-order abstractions.

Recent attempts in psychoanalytic writings to account for subtle qualitative differences in ideational, affective, and motoric behaviors by postulating more and more complex relationships among low-order abstractions, some of them of dubious validity, are felt to represent the perpetuation and elaboration, rather than the amelioration, of the inevitable deficiencies of initial abstractions. Recent acceptance and elaboration of abstractions stating that psychic energy is drawn from a pool or pools, is displaced, is transformed into more refined forms, and is united (i.e., bound) to structures exemplify unparsimonious approaches to explanation, as do related attempts involving concepts of transformation of structures. The concept of equilibration, expressed in mental functioning as facilitation-inhibition, is suggested as an effective organismic concept pitched at a higher level of abstraction—that of control. Exemplification in the present paper of the parsimony, explanatory power, and hypothesis-generating properties of this organismic concept is assumed to have metapsychological merit independent of recent confirmatory findings of neurophysiologists. Recent discoveries concerning neural functioning nevertheless allow the inference that the kind of organismic concept employed here also fits the facts of physical structure and function better than the preliminary abstractions critically examined. It is assumed that ideal explanatory principles for mental functioning are compatible with the facts of both areas.

The present essay includes a critique of certain preliminary abstractions of psychoanalytic theory in a larger attempt to conceptualize ideational, affective, motoric, and related phenomena in terms of equilibration and to spell out implications for temporal and intensity relations among activations at various levels of "columns" of mental activity. This attempt has led to some new hypotheses concerning certain aspects of mental functioning.

[[RunningHead]]

Aeschylus: Oresteia—A Cure in Fifth-Century Athens

Marian Tolpin M.D.

Marian Tolpin

Marian Tolpin, M.D.
360 Jackson Avenue
Glencoe, Ill. 60022

Marian Tolpin  

SUMMARY

Aeschylus transformed and exalted the Orestes legend to dramatize what appear to be hitherto unachieved resolutions of ancient psychological and cultural conflicts. This psychoanalytic study of the Oresteia suggests that:

1. In Athens the resolution of the Greek cultural conflict between ancient goddess cults and later god cults led to a religion which by the fifth century included idealized aspects of both cults. This new religion, institutionalized within their society, required Athenians to adhere to a new level of social morality and to strive to attain new cultural ideals.

2. In what may have been a parallel process on the psychological level, the son renounced ancient strivings to displace the father in an incestuous alliance with the archaic phallic mother and instead came to revere and idealize both parents. As a consequence of this resolution the superego and ego ideal were established as those inner institutions which required the individual to adhere to a new personal morality and to strive to attain new personal ideals.

While Aeschylus used the Orestes legend to dramatize the same tragic conflict immortalized by Sophocles, he also dramatized its successful resolution—a resolution that was of great consequence for the brilliant and enduring achievements of his age.

What Do We Mean by "Id"?

Anne Hayman M.D.

Anne Hayman

Anne Hayman, M.D.
2, Upper Park Road
London, N.W. 3

Anne Hayman  

THIS PAPER GREW OUT OF questions that arose during investigation of a different metapsychological field. I had been asked by a research group with which I am associated to prepare a list of ego functions, which seemed a request for a description of a group of clinical events, either observed or assumed from observations. My first step had been a search through the literature for relevant quotations; apart from a few authors who have offered listings of ego functions, this involved a wider search through the writings of others, to extract from them what seemed to be "ego functions" implied though often not so named. I knew before I started that I would find the term "ego" used in a variety of ways, a point made by a number of writers, e.g., Rapaport (19); and I knew that many, especially the older publications, would need a lot of thought in translating what the authors refer to when they use the term "ego," as clarity in the definitive concept of "ego" would seem an essential prerequisite to deciding what functions could validly be attributed to this instance. (One might put it the other way around, and end up by offering the list of "ego functions" as a way of defining what is meant by "ego" [11].) This seemed a big enough task, but as I read and abstracted it became clear that another problem of definition was involved. [[RunningHead]]

What are Defense Mechanisms?

Richard Siegal Ph.D.

Richard Siegal

Richard S. Siegal, Ph.D.
Box 829
Topeka, Kansas 66601

Richard Siegal  

SUMMARY

The archaic tendency to take a concept as a thing or a substance leads to confusion and unclarity in the theory of defense mechanisms, and inhibits the fullest understanding and classification of them. Defense mechanisms are constructs, man-made abstractions, which may be taken to refer either to mental contents, purposes, outcomes, or to certain processes. In this paper the

806

author attempts to specify the nature and sequential organization of these processes as he describes defense mechanisms as ways of relating one's self, one's consciousness, and one's body to inner stimuli.

A Common Type of Marital Incompatibility

Sidney Levin M.D.

Sidney Levin

Sidney Levin, M.D.
1265 Beacon Street
Brookline, Mass. 02146

Sidney Levin  

SUMMARY

A husband's depression often arises in response to his wife's ego-syntonic patterns of rejecting his masculine position. It is proposed that these patterns of response in the wife, which are often denied by her, usually reflect her unconscious resistance to the mastery of shame affect. This resistance is typically supported by a pathological attitude of "narcissistic entitlement" which has been camouflaged through a variety of defenses, including the use of denial.

Discussion

Charles Brenner M.D.

Charles Brenner

Charles Brenner, M.D.
1040 Park Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10028

Charles Brenner  

IN 1915 Freud introduced the term "metapsychology" with the following words: "We see how we have gradually been led into adopting a third point of view in our account of physical phenomena. Besides the dynamic and the topographical points of view, we have adopted the economic one. This endeavours to follow out the vicissitudes of amounts of excitation and to arrive at least at some relative estimate of their magnitude.

"It will not be unreasonable to give a special name to this way of regarding our subject-matter, for it is the consummation of psycho-analytic research. I propose that when we have succeeded in describing a psychical process in its dynamic, topographical and economic aspects, we should speak of it as a metapsychological presentation" (11, p. 181).

Webster (1953) defines "metapsychology" as having to do with the relationship between mind and body. Since "quantities of excitation" refer to the energies of the instinctual (and, in 1915, of the self-preservative, or ego) drives, a concept which Freud considered to be on the borderline between mind and body, it is possible that the dictionary definition just cited influenced his choice of the term "metapsychology." However, it is by no means certain that this is the case. "Metapsychology" is not apparently a widely used word, except in the field of psychoanalysis, since it is listed neither in the shorter Oxford English Dictionary (1933) nor in its American counterpart twenty years later. [[RunningHead]]

Dreams and Psychosis

John Mack M.D.

John Mack

John Mack  

In opening the panel John Frosch explained that it had been necessary to limit the panels to half a day, as a result some of the presentations would have to be shortened. In order to provide an overview of the subject Frosch presented his paper, "A Survey of Psychoanalytic Contributions to the Relationship between Dreams and Psychosis." He pointed out that Freud, as many others had done before him, drew frequent analogies between dreams and psychosis in his writings. He recalled the passage from The Interpretation of Dreams (1900) in which Freud described three possible relations between dreams and mental disorders. These were: (i) etiological and clinical connections, as when a dream represents a psychotic state or introduces it or is left over from it; (ii) modifications to which dream life is subject in cases of mental disease; and (iii) intrinsic connections between the structure of dreams and psychosis.

Initially in 1900 Freud stressed the similarity between dreams and psychosis, emphasizing that the principal difference was between the harmless nightly relaxation of censorship that occurs in the former and the pathological reduction of censorship, or overwhelming intensification of unconscious excitation, in the latter. Frosch observed that it is difficult to keep one's bearings in Freud's discussions of the subject. He adhered more strictly, for example, to the analogy between dreams and psychosis in some of his writings than in others. Freud also tried to summarize at various times the differences between dreams and mental disorders, suggesting that in dreams a withdrawal of cathexis might affect all systems equally, whereas in schizophrenia, for example, only cathexis of the system Ucs. would be withdrawn. Frosch described further how Freud had attempted unsuccessfully to distinguish dreams and various types of mental disorders on the basis of the topographical theory. What emerged from this attempt was the clear difficulty of seeking to differentiate dreams and various types of mental disorders on the basis of the distribution of libido among the various systems within this model.

[[RunningHead]]

The Cryptomnesic Fragment in the Discovery of Free Association

Harry Trosman M.D.

Harry Trosman

Harry Trosman, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry University of Chicago
950 59 Street
Chicago, Ill. 60637

Harry Trosman  

SUMMARY

Cryptomnesia occasionally accompanies an original discovery in a highly creative person. A number of such cryptomnesic phenomena are described, and particular attention is paid to Ludwig Börne's influence on Freud's discovery of free association. The discovery is seen as the fulfillment of an adaptive task consistent with the scientific path Freud was pursuing. The cryptomnesic fragment, although related to conflict and thus an ideational remnant

508

of a failure of repression, is also an indicator of the link between the creator and his intellectual and personal tradition in the form of a creative alliance. The period of early adolescence, when ideational content is patterned by narcissistically derived ideals, is suggested as critical to the potentially creative individual in that bonds of attachment to the historical past permit acceptance of creative powers and give direction for potential investments. Figures for identification are likely to follow a design established by an assessment of specifically unique attributes. Börne was not only a minor source for the discovery of free association but also an early precursor of "the unseen collaborator" necessitated by the hazards of revolutionary creative achievement.

[[RunningHead]]

On the Nature of Addiction to Cigarettes

Eli Marcovitz M.D.

Eli Marcovitz

Eli Marcovitz, M.D.
255 South 17 Street
Philadelphia, Pa. 19103

Eli Marcovitz  

SUMMARY

This paper attempts to explain some of the factors which make it difficult for heavy cigarette smokers to give up smoking, or to switch to cigars or pipe, in spite of the general recognition of the increased risk to their physical health and life expectancy which accompanies heavy cigarette smoking.

Heretofore, all forms of smoking have been usually considered an oral addiction, deriving from the constellation of needs, pleasures, and conflicts relating to the mouth and its activities, sucking, biting, swallowing, spitting, etc.

I contend that heavy cigarette smoking is characterized and differentiated from other forms of smoking by its dependence on inhalation. Exhalation and visualization of the smoke are important components of what I designate as the respiratory triad characterizing heavy cigarette smoking. By means of these three

1094

processes, the smoker attempts to accomplish any of a number of the following unconscious purposes:

1. In the attempt to delimit the body image in the quest for the sense of self, to know the inner boundaries of the body is as important as knowing the outer boundaries. The irritating quality of smoke enables one to perceive more acutely the interior of the chest cavity. To feel the interior of the chest is for some the closest to feeling the interior of the self.

2. There is a pleasure component to breathing, which for many people is enhanced by the increased sensory stimulation in the inhalation of cigarette smoke.

3. Heavy smokers smoke from the moment they wake to the moment they go to sleep; coughs, colds, emphysema, nothing can stop them. For some, the more difficulty in breathing, the more they need the cigarette. For them the cigarette is like the scuba diver's air-line to his tank. They need it in order to breathe, to relieve the unconscious fear of suffocation.

4. Throughout human history, in various primitive cultures, the inhalation of smoke was a symbolic-magic method of incorporating the spirit of a god by whom one was then possessed, sharing in his omnipotence. For some, the inhalation of cigarette smoke similarly endows them with magic powers, especially to win love objects or to conquer adversaries.

5. The amorphous quality of the exhaled smoke lends itself to be experienced unconsciously as an externalization of the self, the embodiment of one's own spirit, and the proof of immortality. Closely related to this is the use of the smoke as a screen on which to project unconscious fantasies.

Because heavy cigarette smoking is primarily a respiratory addiction with these unconscious purposes being fulfilled by way of the respiratory triad, other oral substitutive activities, including the use of pipe or cigar, are not sufficiently helpful in the attempt to stop. In order to stop, some form of respiratory substitute such as deep-breathing or breath-holding exercises may help, provided there is a real inner conviction that instead of being a fantasied source of power and life as well as pleasure, cigarette smoking is a real danger.

[[RunningHead]]

Sensory Irruptions and Mental Organization

Theodore Lipin M.D.

Theodore Lipin

Theodore Lipin, M.D.
11 East 87 Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

Theodore Lipin  

SUMMARY

Irruptions into the inner world of sensory elements which convey nothing meaningful to the subject occur regularly. They are characteristic of normal mental functioning and, during sleep, may be a source of dreams.

The irruptions are generated by the activity of repressed mental processes and can influence the functioning of the integrated part of the mind.

The repressed activity of split-off portions of the mind sometimes primarily utilizes secondary processes, bound energy, and regulation by the reality principle, and sometimes primary processes, mobile energy, and regulation by the pleasure-unpleasure principle. The secondary process type of cognition that is utilized, has a quality that is phase-specific for some prior period during which the splitting off occurred.

Splitting off of organized functional segments regularly occurs under usual conditions in a usual, constructive way that is a part of the maturational process. It also occurs under unusual conditions in an unusual, pathogenic way that is a part of the defensive process against trauma or unpleasure.

The activity of the split-off portions of the mind may increase the disturbances caused by pathological processes; it also may serve as an instrumentality of reparative processes and be a component of adaptive and creative functioning.

[[RunningHead]]

Discussion

Nathaniel Ross M.D.

Nathaniel Ross

Nathaniel Ross, M.D.
830 Park Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10021

Nathaniel Ross  

Protagonists of attempts to improve and revise psychoanalytic theory have an illustrious predecessor—Sigmund Freud. While such attempts in the hands of the so-called "deviant schools," "culturalists," "existentialist psychoanalysts," et al. have not infrequently drawn attention to neglected, or not yet arrived-at aspects of psychological problems, they have for the most part wandered off into the sterile sands of a consciousness psychology, or the morass of mystical vagaries.

With the latter-day critics who consist of psychologists thoroughly trained and grounded not only in their own discipline, which is far more rigorous and exacting than most psychoanalysts are aware of, but also in psychoanalysis, it is an entirely different matter. Such efforts must, to my mind, be looked upon by the analytic world with enthusiasm, for I have no doubt that if psychoanalysis is to grow as a science, analysts must cooperate fully with their fellows in psychology, such as the author of this paper. Others, of course, include such prolific contributors as George Klein, Robert Holt, Roy Schafer, Margaret Brenman, as well as the unique Erik Erikson. We all know, too, how David Rapaport advanced our knowledge of the theory of ego psychology. Within the ranks of psychoanalysts themselves, there are a few working toward the revision of the theory in the light of information theory and the advancing discoveries in neurophysiology. Whatever comes of all these attempts, such efforts must not be greeted with casual or polemical dismissal.

Submitted May 26, 1967

Closing Comments

Riley Gardner Ph.D.

Riley Gardner

Riley W. Gardner, Ph.D.
The Menninger Foundation
Topeka, Kansas 66601

Riley Gardner  

Perhaps the most effective brief method of responding to these extended discussions is to reiterate and elaborate upon the major purposes and implications of the essay. To do so may clarify some of the major issues raised by the three discussants. Let me express in advance my indebtedness to the discussants for the careful consideration they have given to the points made in the essay.

My major thesis is that certain aspects of individuality of personality organization can now be conceptualized in terms of controlling structures, and that these structures (i.e., these enduring patterns of mental operations) can be viewed as the principal means by which the human organism carries out an evolutionary edict to maintain overall equilibration within certain adaptive limits. These proposals are intended to embed the theory in an organismic framework, to raise the level of explanation of certain phenomena to a more abstract level, and to increase the parsimony of the theory. In offering these suggestions, I have argued that the complex energy-structure duality understandably employed in earlier conceptualizations can now be recast in terms of a more unitary conception—that of controlling structures governing the facilitation-inhibition of mental processes that may vary in level of activation. In support of this argument, I drew upon recent developments in neurophysiology that seem to provide further knowledge of the kinds of neural mechanisms involved in facilitation-inhibition.

Submitted February 14, 1968

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Pleasure Principle, the Constancy Principle, and the Primary Autonomous Ego

William Needles M.D.

William Needles

William Needles, M.D.
45 East 82 Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

William Needles  

SUMMARY

The constancy principle states that the psychic apparatus strives to avoid excitation of all sorts, whether from within or without the organism. Since certain observations at all stages of psychic development seemed to indicate a seeking for certain modes of stimulation of various external sense organs, with no apparent evidence of concomitant discharge, a re-examination of the principle was undertaken. Schneirla's biological discovery of a universal tendency in all animals to approach as well as to withdraw from stimuli was considered for its relevance to the problem, as was the existence and functioning early in life of a primary autonomous ego, operating with its own energies. Reasons were adduced for a revision of the principle to include a seeking of pleasure, pleasure being equated in energic terms with certain modes of external excitation. Thus, while Freud, confronted by the phenomenon of pleasurable excitation, admitted total defeat or sought to salvage the theory by invoking ad hoc alternation in rate or rhythm of energic changes, and while Schur attempts—unsuccessfully, in my opinion—to solve the problem by summarily banishing feelings from consideration, the present thesis takes the view that feelings and energy level can be correlated, but not simplistically, when simple, elemental internal or external stimulation is involved. A rise in instinctual drive tension can be equated with unpleasure and a diminution with satisfaction; but external stimulation results in either pleasure or unpleasure, depending on the modality involved.

It is suggested (i) that since the psychic apparatus seeks excitation as well as discharge, the constancy principle, which allows only for the latter, should be abandoned; (ii) that if an energic principle is retained, it should be one that allows for both processes; (iii) that the pleasure principle, originally formulated by Freud as the pursuit of pleasure and the avoidance of unpleasure, should retain this meaning and not be expressed in energic terms.

Herbert Weiner  

The mind-brain problem, despite its long history, remains one of the most complex, urgent, and unsolved problems in philosophy and science. It has engaged the best minds throughout history; but it is a problem to which not even Freud found a solution. Since his time, and in spite of major empirical advances in neurobiology and the best efforts of some of the finest minds of the twentieth century, very little progress toward its solution has been made. Anyone who tackles the task of unraveling the relationship between mind and brain does so at his peril, forewarned by others' past failures, directed by certain guidelines, and with the knowledge that his efforts will be rewarded by the appreciation of those with less courage.

Various model solutions to the problem have been proposed, for example, isomorphism and psychophysical parallelism. A less popular but recurrent solution is the identity theory of which Gardner's paper is a representative example. And as such his paper exemplifies some of the difficulties with which the identity model is fraught.

At one period of his thought, Carnap, for example, held that the language of all sciences could be reduced to a unitary basis. However, he later abandoned this belief.

Professor of Psychiatry, Albert Einstein College of Medicine; and Associate Chief, Division of Psychiatry, Montefiore Hospital and Medical Center, New York.

Submitted February 14, 1967

[[RunningHead]]

Spider Symbolism and Bisexuality

Lawrence Newman M.D. and Robert Stoller M.D.

Robert Stoller

Lawrence E. Newman, M.D.
Robert J. Stoller, M.D.
U.C.L.A. School of Medicine, Department of Psychiatry
Los Angeles, California 90024

Robert Stoller  

SUMMARY

We have reviewed the literature on spider symbolism and have especially noted how the spider is used to symbolize conflict at both the oral and phallic level. We have studied a patient who confirms Abraham's observation that the spider symbolizes bisexual genitalia, "the penis within the female sex organs." She is a female with hermaphroditic genitalia, who during a psychotic episode developed the idea that her genitalia were a spider. We found that the spider also represented the patient's mother and the patient herself as a hermaphrodite. The restitutive function of the spider symbol as a representation of the self was discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

Further Comments on a Common Type of Marital Incompatibility

Sidney Levin M.D.

Sidney Levin

Sidney Levin, M.D.
72 Evans Road
Brookline, Mass. 02146

Sidney Levin  

SUMMARY

In many instances a vicious circle of marital incompatibility results from a tendency on the part of the husband, when frustrated sexually by his wife, to react by withdrawing from her and by developing an attitude of bitter grievance, which prevents a reconciliation. Some aspects of the sensitivity which underlies these reactions have been discussed. Since such sensitivity is frequently equated in the unconscious with deficient masculinity, it tends to evoke unconscious shame, which in turn may lead to a denial of the sensitivity. Successful treatment must therefore include a careful analysis of the unconscious shame and of the denial of sensitivity to which it gives rise, so that the underlying sensitivity can be exposed and the resulting withdrawal can eventually be reversed.

Some Aspects of Charcot's Influence on Freud

Julian Miller M.D.

Julian Miller

Julian A. Miller, M.D.
Melvin Sabshin, M.D.
912 South Wood Street
Chicago, Ill. 60612
John E. Gedo, M.D.
George H. Pollock, M.D.
Leo Sadow, M.D.
Nathan Schlessinger, M.D.

Julian Miller  

IN PREVIOUS PAPERS (15), (22), (23) members of the Workshop on Scientific Methodology have considered the origins and modifications of some early concepts in psychoanalysis. We had been interested in the influences impinging on Freud during the early days of discovery and concept formation. In this context, Freud's trip to Paris to study with Charcot seemed pivotal in his shift from neurological and physiological to psychopathological work. Although Jones (19) traces the principles upon which Freud constructed his theories back to the influence of Brücke, he also states: "It was assuredly the experience with Charcot in Paris that aroused Freud's interest in hysteria, then in psychopathology in general, and so paved the way for resuscitating Breuer's observation and developing psychoanalysis" (Vol. 1, p. 75).

We have examined Charcot's writings (3) as well as some of those who have commented upon his work (5), (16), (17), (18), (24) to understand Charcot's theoretical perspective, his clinical technique, his research methods, aspects of his personality, and to assess the relative impact of each of these factors upon Freud.

Charcot the Theorist

Charcot recognized hysteria as a mental phenomenon and formulated a specific etiological theory for it (cf. 24).

Submitted September 8, 1967

[[RunningHead]]

Problems of Termination in the Analysis of Adults

Stephen Firestein M.D.

Stephen Firestein

Stephen Firestein  

In his introduction to the Panel Samuel A. Guttman reminded the audience that the panels of the meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association have continuously maintained an important and popular role. During the two decades in which panels on various subjects have been conducted they have varied somewhat in their format between largely prepared presentations, at one extreme, and briefer papers with spontaneous and lively interchange among the panelists, and increased audience participation, at the other.

Guttman highlighted the fact that our most creative and experienced colleagues in the course of time tend gradually and naturally to limit the degree of their active participation in our meetings. Impressed with the fact that psychoanalysis is an area of endeavor demanding human qualities acquired only through many years of living, Guttman sought a way to permit us to learn as much as possible from these most experienced colleagues. So many items of valuable clinical experience never attain publication in the literature.

Out of conversations with Peter Neubauer, Guttman developed an idea which was proposed to the Program Committee: an investigative commission, a group of persons invited to perform certain specific tasks.

In the context of this Panel a number of psychoanalysts from different geographical areas were invited to participate by arranging to meet locally with some of their senior psychoanalytic colleagues. The task focused upon was to make inquiry regarding problems of termination in the analysis of adults, with special attention to two specific questions:

1. What is the relationship of the patient (candidate and other analysands) to the analyst after the analysis is terminated?

2. How does the anticipation of what will constitute this postanalytic relationship influence the conduct of the analysis, especially in the termination phase?

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Boston, May 11, 1968. Chairman: Samuel A. Guttman, M.D.

[[RunningHead]]

The Theory of Genital Primacy in the Light of Ego Psychology

Martin Berezin M.D.

Martin Berezin

Martin Berezin  

This panel was organized and designed with the intent of re-examining one of the oldest, accepted psychoanalytic concepts. In his introduction to the panel, Nathaniel Ross stated that the concept of genital primacy appears to be one of the most solid bastions in the structure of psychoanalytic theory, yet very little appears in the literature which represents an attempt to revise, reappraise, or reinvestigate it, let alone challenge it. It has long been taken for granted that the optimal kind of sexual experience should be inevitably linked to the optimal kind of human relationship, the abiding, steadfast capacity for postambivalent relationships between the sexes. Thus, the attainment of mature object relations and of genital primacy have traditionally been considered dependent variables.

More often the reverse aspect of genital primacy is familiar to psychoanaysts in their work with patients, viz., that neurotic men and women are incapable of satisfactory experiences in the sexual act. Wilhelm Reich claimed that this was true in all cases of neurosis.

As Ross pointed out, throughout the literature the beneficent effects of orgastic experiences are spelled out—freedom from guilt, freedom to sublimate, strengthening of the ego, optimal relief of tension, regression in the service of the ego, optimal distribution of cathexes, strengthening of mutuality between the sexes, strengthening of personal integrations, favorable influences upon the offspring, relief of pregenital tensions in subserving the genital function, etc. In addition, it has been stated that very possibly the height of human joy is attained in a mutual orgastic experience by a man and woman who feel tenderness and unambivalent love for each other.

On Raskolnikov's Dreams in Dostoyevsky's Crime and Punishment

Richard Lower M.D.

Richard Lower

Richard B. Lower, M.D.
430 Morris Avenue
Bryn Mawr, Pa. 19010

Richard Lower  

SUMMARY

Analysis of Raskolnikov's dreams reveals the murder of the old woman to be the defensive acting out of an unconscious sadomasochistic oedipal fantasy. In the murder he identified with the castrating father in order to avoid his anxiety in identifying with the passive, castrated mother, and with the axe he actively attempted to destroy the projected passive-feminine part of himself. The effort failed because, in addition, the woman unconsciously represented his dead father, and the resulting guilt forced him back into a vicious guilt-shame cycle.

Thus, Crime and Punishment, which would seem to explore the theme of matricide, takes its place alongside The Brothers Karamazov as a product of Dostoyevsky's attempt to resolve his own unconscious patricidal conflict.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lady Macbeth and Infanticide or "How Many Children had Lady Macbeth" Murdered?

Victor Calef M.D.

Victor Calef

Victor Calef, M.D.
2618 Jackson Street
San Francisco, Calif. 94115

Victor Calef  

SUMMARY

Freud was dissatisfied with his analysis of Macbeth, though he continued to be convinced that it concerned itself with childlessness, a psychological punishment for the crime against the laws of geniture. The concept, typically talion, remains an uncontested psychological construct, probably because it is readily demonstrable clinically. Little attention has been paid to the fact that the psychological aspects of childlessness have not been

545

completely explored, and that Freud apparently dropped his interest in that theme at the same time that he expressed his dissatisfaction with his analysis of Macbeth. That which he felt was his unproven thesis has been widely accepted, and childlessness has not been extensively investigated analytically. Freud experienced his effort as incomplete, even though he went on to give a brilliant demonstration of the central role played by the prohibition against the fulfillment of oedipal wishes in Ibsen's Rosmersholm.

The analysis of a patient who insisted on an abortion (infanticide), and who resisted the analytic process by destroying what it produced, called attention to the fact that one of Lady Macbeth's exhortations is accompanied by the boast of having experienced a suckling child at her breast whose boneless gums she would willingly tear from her breast and dash his brains out. Though the Macbeths were said to be childless, the childlessness and the boast have not been linked, and neither Shakespeare nor Freud tried to resolve the apparent contradiction in the two ideas. It must be admitted that in history, legend, and play, Lady Macbeth's childlessness may refer only to her marriage to Macbeth, and her experiences of a suckling child are then readily understandable by the recognition of a previous marriage without invoking deeper psychological themes. Yet her willingness to kill a child for the sake of founding a dynasty does seem to need some further explanation.

Clinical experience suggests that childlessness is sometimes unconsciously equated with abortion and infanticide and may be representative of a defensive wish on the part of the ego in its efforts to deal with the oedipal triumphs which then have to be destroyed. The material from the clinical case called attention to the possible importance of Lady Macbeth's exhortation and boast for the explanation of her illness. And that sequence from the clinical to the literary themes suggested that perhaps Freud, too, always came to, or returned to, the literary themes only after he had been able to obtain clinical evidence from his patients; and that despite the fact that his ideas about childlessness and Lady Macbeth were correct, he stated them in a manner (childlessness as a talion punishment) which was

546

unsatisfactory to him primarily because he did not have the clinical evidence so necessary to his style and which he needed to be clear for himself that the wish to destroy the child was a wish of the ego used to hide the more heinous oedipal crime. It is tempting to hypothesize that until the time of his attempted analysis of Macbeth Freud's patients had portrayed for him only that they conceived of childlessness as punishment; that he had not had any cases in which childlessness was experienced as a crime to hide a crime in the same way he had reconstructed in his concept of a criminal out of a sense of guilt. Even if he had had no clinical case of the kind described, it does seem obvious from his statements about the crimes against the laws of geniture that he must have suspected the existence of fantasies of infanticide in history, legend, and play.

It is not necessary to reconcile in a psychological sense the actions and the themes of history, legend, and play, even if the legend and play are determined by history, as Shakespeare's Macbeth was. Yet the recognition of child murder and abortion (infanticide) as the central psychological theme of the play permits an adequate reconciliation of the three as well as offering a glimpse into an interest in childlessness shared by both Shakespeare and Freud.

The Reversal of Déjà Raconté

Dale Boesky M.D.

Dale Boesky

Dale Boesky, M.D.
26789 Woodward Avenue
Huntington Woods, Michigan 48070

Dale Boesky  

SUMMARY

The reversal of déjà raconté occurs when a patient erroneously asserts that he has not already divulged something to the analyst. Clinical material is presented to demonstrate that this reversal is a transference resistance rooted in castration anxiety, which is designed to repudiate degraded self representations.

The Problem of Character Theory

Martin Stein M.D.

Martin Stein

Martin H. Stein, M.D.
8 East 96 Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

Martin Stein  

… the processes of the formation of character are more obscure and less accessible to analysis than neurotic ones (Freud, 1913, p. 323).

Sixty years ago, the theory of symptom formation reached a stage of elegance and inclusiveness which has so far been surpassed by no other clinical theory in our field. In an eight-page paper with the deceptive title, "Hysterical Phantasies and Their Relation to Bisexuality" (1908a), Freud summarized what he had discovered up to that point, and managed to anticipate most of what was to be written of symptom formation during the succeeding sixty years. His introduction to the section which embodies his ideas is worth quoting:

For the sake of general interest I will at this point go outside the framework of this paper and interpolate a series of formulas which attempt to give a progressively fuller description of the nature of hysterical symptoms. These formulas do not contradict one another, but some represent an increasingly complete and precise approach to the facts, while others represent the application of different points of view:

1. Hysterical symptoms are mnemic symbols of certain operative (traumatic) impressions and experiences.

2. Hysterical symptoms are substitutes, produced by 'conversions', for the associative return of these traumatic experiences.

Presented at the Plenary Session of the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, December 22, 1968.

Submitted March 5, 1969

[[RunningHead]]

Dreams about the Patient—An Approach to the Problem of Countertransference

Roy Whitman M.D. , Milton Kramer M.D. and Bill Baldridge Ph.D.

Bill Baldridge

Roy M. Whitman, M.D.
Milton Kramer, M.D.
Bill J. Baldridge, Ph.D.
University of Cincinnati College of Medicine Cincinnati General Hospital
Cincinnati, Ohio 45229

Bill Baldridge  

SUMMARY

Current research into the process of dreaming has shown that dreams occur much more often in all human beings than was hitherto suspected. The analyst or therapist is no exception to this finding. Among the dreams of the therapist are dreams about the patient. Dreaming about the patient in the manifest content of a dream is one form of countertransference dream. The percentage of the total dreams of therapists is probably not much different than that which can be deduced from Freud's original book of The Interpretation of Dreams, i.e., 6 percent of the therapist's dreams have patients in the manifest content and 34 percent have patients in the latent content as well.

The various sources that are examined for dreams about patients by therapists are: (i) Freud's dreams; (ii) therapists' dreams about patients experimentally recovered; (iii) dreams about patients by psychiatric residents in supervision; (iv) dreams about patients of analysts as candidates; (v) dreams about patients by psychiatric residents in treatment; (vi) dreams about patients by therapists not in treatment or supervision; and (vii) articles containing references to therapists' dreams about patients.

The scrutiny of dreams about the patient in the course of supervision and analysis has been invariably helpful in producing insights into the doctor-patient relationship. Just as dreams are the royal road to the unconscious of the patient, so are they equally useful in providing a succinct idea of the therapist's attitude to his patient.

Dreams may be used for an investigation of countertransference phenomena. The overall spirit of this study is that countertransference phenomena, rather than being seen as interference, can be used in a creative way to illuminate the treatment process. An important differentiation is the difference between countertransference reactions and countertransference neurosis. A countertransference neurosis, i.e., a persistent neurotic attitude about a patient, is reflected in persistent dreams about that patient. Countertransference

725

reactions or attitudes are seen in occasional dreams about the patient.

The physical composition of the panel was somewhat modified due to the unexpected disablement, by the flu, of three of the members, Leo Rangell, the panel chairman, Jacob A. Arlow, and Henry Brosin. Elizabeth R. Zetzel chaired the panel on Rangell's behalf. He and Arlow were further represented by papers forwarded to the panel. Brosin's presentation was omitted regrettably, because it involved slide-film projections demonstrating dyssynchronous motor activity in schizophrenics and required a running narration by the author.

Zetzel read a chairman's introductory position paper prepared for the panel by Leo Rangell. Calling our attention to the considerable interest which exists in this subject today both from the theoretical and clinical standpoints, Rangell began by citing the classical contribution of Karl Bühler, whose tripatite classification in 1934 differentiated the functions of language into (a) expression, i.e., of internal needs; (b) appeal, to external objects; and (c) description, of events, objects, etc., i.e., the cognitive function. This succinct formulation, which has since been widely quoted by psychologists and psychoanalysts alike, was utilized by Loewenstein in describing the various functions of speech in analysis. Spitz found it helpful in describing the ontogenesis of the first meaningful symbolic gestures of "no" and "yes" in infancy, and Rangell, looking beyond the "no" and "yes" gestures to the origins of the words themselves, had pointed out previously that while the acquisition of the gestures carries the infant from the expressive to the appeal phase, the achievement of the words themselves carries communication to the third or descriptive level. Psychoanalysis, by adding to Bühler's germinal insight the role of unconscious factors, brought new dimensions to our understanding of "internal needs," of the affective and cognitive processes involved, and of the complex interrelationships between

[[RunningHead]]

Narcissistic Resistance

Nathan Segel M.D.

Nathan Segel

Nathan Segel  

Charles Kligerman opened the meeting by pointing out that the current panel was an outgrowth of a panel on narcissism which had been chaired by Maxwell Gitelson in 1961. Although the main emphasis of the present panel would be on clinical aspects, he hoped it could be set in an effective theoretical framework. The very subject matter, "Narcissistic Resistance," was seen to be the confluence of two complex and often obscure concepts. Further, it was only fairly recently that there had been any specific articulation of the idea of narcissistic defenses as such, although various aspects have in the past been dealt with as part of pregenital regressions or the character defenses of Wilhelm Reich. Karl Abraham perhaps made the first unequivocal mention of it in 1919. It was interesting that Freud omitted it not only from his classical list of resistances (1926), but also from any explicit place in "Analysis Terminable and Interminable." Freud may have neglected the defensive uses of narcissism because his concept had arisen from his clinical interest in the problems of homosexuality and psychoses, so that it was reserved mainly for the graver conditions. What did characterize Freud's views on narcissism were all-or-none dichotomies and polarities as witness the narcissistic neuroses vs. the transference neuroses; narcissistic libido vs. object libido; primary vs. secondary narcissism, etc. Normal narcissism was an early developmental stage to be surmounted or else seen as regressive. It was originally described in energic terms as a quantitative economic concept in spite of its obvious structural, genetic, and dynamic considerations. Federn (1929) was given credit for some of the earliest stress on "the healthy aspects of narcissism.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1968. Chairman: Charles Kligerman, M.D.

SUMMARY

Certain trends or notions of teaching and learning are utilized in order to develop a blueprint for the study of psychoanalytic pedagogy. The dynamics of psychoanalytic professional and scientific education are characterized by special psychological features of our traditional institute structure. Of particular concern is the nature of the supervisory structure as well as overidealistic expectations of our work.

[[RunningHead]]

Unconscious Fantasy and Masturbatory Technique

Ira Miller M.D.

Ira Miller

Ira Miller, M.D.
1718 Hermitage Road
Ann Arbor, Mich. 48104

Ira Miller  

SUMMARY

It has been emphasized that in certain episodes of masturbation, especially those without conscious fantasy or those in which the conscious fantasy does not prove illuminating, analysis of the specific masturbatory technique may lead to the uncovering of the unconscious fantasy. This is due to the fact that the unconscious fantasy may be acted out via the masturbating technique. In the analysis, such techniques are treated as symptoms or, more accurately, as though they were manifest dream contents. It has been stressed that, more often than not, the patient will omit such details in the service of resistance, and that the patient must be trained as to their importance by the analyst's questioning. Clinical examples have been presented in illustration of this premise. Finally, a question was raised as to the possibility that the assumed universality of beating fantasies may manifest itself through the physical act of masturbation itself.

Superior Intelligence—Its Contribution to Neurogenesis

Sylvan Keiser M.D.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser, M.D.
1100 Madison Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10028

Sylvan Keiser  

SUMMARY

Superior intellectual endowment had an adverse effect on the psychological development of several patients. In childhood, they regarded themselves as "exceptions" and they were so regarded by their environment. Precocious intelligence gave them access to information and understanding beyond their chronological age. The impact of their insights overwhelmed their immature egos, thus releasing libidinal and aggressive fantasies. This imposed further burdens on their developing egos. In the patients studied, the functioning of their intelligence was constricted. The greater sensitivity to stimuli required stronger methods to master the resultant excitation. It is suggested that for some patients markedly superior intelligence facilitates traumatic experiences which then lead to a neurosis.

[[RunningHead]]

Hypnosis as a Method for Investigating Unconscious Thought Processes—A Review of Research

Lawrence Stross M.D. and Howard Shevrin Ph.D.

Howard Shevrin

Lawrence Stross, M.D.
Howard Shevrin, Ph.D.
3535 Cherry Creek N. Drive
Denver, Col. 80209

Howard Shevrin  

SUMMARY

For some time, the value of hypnosis as a tool for scientific investigation was obscured by its dramatic therapeutic effects. Hypnosis played a critical role in revealing the relationship between unconscious thinking and neurotic symptoms by providing Freud with a microscope of the psyche to observe what was heretofore unobservable—the traumatic memories with their strange connections to symptoms. Unfortunately, as the limitations of the cathartic method became evident, hypnoanalysis was abandoned by Freud and with it interest was lost in using hypnosis as a method for investigating mental processes. Our historical review of studies involving hypnosis was organized around and focused attention on two points: the notable methodological advances and theoretical contributions that came out of the Breuer and Freud clinical innovation of listening to patients talk freely while in hypnosis, and what we delineated as a basic pattern or schema between apparently widely divergent and unrelated observations and studies, with particular emphasis on the mediating role of language.

Hypnosis may have been superseded as a therapy, but the advantages in using hypnosis as a research instrument remain. By examining in detail, as we have in this paper, the clinical observations and experiments using hypnosis through the years, one can better appreciate the striking role and unique part played by hypnosis in making available otherwise unknown relationships between such things as posthypnotic suggestion phenomena, dreams, symptom formation, and certain experimentally induced behavior. Without the purchase provided by hypnotically retrieved reminiscences, it is unlikely that the nature of unconscious

133

processes following different rules of thought as compared to those of conscious processes could have as easily been discovered and identified by Freud. Despite some of the limitations of the studies described in this paper, when reviewed together they form an impressive series in which the findings are generally positive and suggest many further research possibilities. We described our own use of subliminal stimuli with multiple levels of potential associative responses as an experimental method that appears to hold promise as a systematic, controlled means of studying the nature of thinking in hypnosis and other states of consciousness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference Birth Fantasies and Narcissism

Gilbert Rose M.D.

Gilbert Rose

Gilbert J. Rose, M.D.
1 Highland Ave.
Rowayton, Conn. 06853

Gilbert Rose  

SUMMARY

Over an additional period of five years, nine more cases were found to show patterns of unconscious birth fantasies centering around the end of the ninth month of treatment. The criteria for their selection are described. They had to show at least five indicators of birth fantasies within six weeks, plus or minus, of the end of the ninth month of treatment. The indicators were manifest dream content, dream symbolism, anal symbolism, and overt and, in the case of two males, covert references to birth. An absence from treatment for a week or more near the end of the ninth month may often represent the acting out of a birth fantasy and was taken as a presumptive indicator of the presence of a birth fantasy. More than half of the indicators centered within two weeks, plus or minus, of the end of the ninth month of treatment. Case examples are given of the various meanings of the birth fantasies. They included preoedipal separation problems, oedipal transferences, penis envy, jealousy of the female's reproductive ability, and attempts at mastery of trauma and fear of death. All of these categories of meaning may subserve attempts at

1028

correcting a narcissistic disequilibrium. Unconscious birth fantasies at the end of the ninth month of treatment might be viewed as accompaniments of thrusts toward individuation as well as defenses against regressive narcissistic fusion.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis—Taught, Learned, and Experienced

Sylvan Keiser M.D.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser, M.D.
1100 Madison Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10028

Sylvan Keiser  

In this essay the term "psychoanalysis" will be used to designate that method for the study of the human mind which is based on the principles and techniques originated and elaborated by Sigmund Freud. It will embrace contributions made by students of Freud since his earliest revolutionary discoveries. Contributions which have deepened insight and expanded understanding have also been added to the definition of the word. Studies which have done violence to the basic principles of psychoanalysis are not covered by this definition. It has often been a source of wonderment that people who disown or discard or attack with virulence the basic principles of Freudian psychoanalysis persist in the use of the word "psychoanalysis" for their types of therapy. Such a term should be anathema for them.

Under the term psychoanalysis employed in this text, the Freudian analytic procedure is conducted so that therapeutic results are ideally a byproduct of the search for knowledge and self-understanding. This is true even though the patient comes seeking a cure through analysis.

Division of Psychoanalytic Education, Downstate Medical Center, Brooklyn, N.Y., and Brookdale Hospital Center, Brooklyn, N.Y.

Kurt R. Eissler: MEDICAL ORTHODOXY AND THE FUTURE OF PSYCHOANALYSIS. New York: International Universities Press, 1965.

responseSet.title: Schnitzler's Das Schicksal Des Freiherrn Von Leisenbohg
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Schnitzler's Das Schicksal Des Freiherrn Von Leisenbohg

M. Katan M.D.

M. Katan

M. Katan, M.D.
2550 Arlington Road
Cleveland Heights, Ohio 44118

M. Katan  

SUMMARY

I became interested in Schnitzler's story long before I started my analytic career. Later I made the acquaintance of Freud's remark that the "taboo of virginity" formed the core of Das Schicksal des Freiherrn von Leisenbohg. I also took note of Sachs's and Reik's futile attempts to throw light on this story. Driven perhaps by an urge to clarify the feelings aroused in me when I read this story for the first time, I wrote an analysis of it in 1944. Recently I produced a new version of this analysis.

A friend of mine criticized this new version, claiming I had treated Schnitzler's personages as if they possessed a well-developed human mind, a supposition which, according to my friend, was not valid. My critic friend added that, as a result of my viewpoint, Schnitzler acquired the status of a great writer, a status which he did not deserve. Schnitzler, it is true, could have made better use of his unconscious in building up the dramatic situation which he examined, namely, the influence of a dying man's curse. This curse led to a chain of events consisting of the interactions of the various dramatis personae of the

924

story and culminating in the death of the Freiherr. The analysis revealed that each action resulted from the unconscious conflict of one of the contributing persons. Schnitzler not only showed himself a brilliant psychologist, but, in addition, in this story (published in 1903) he described such concepts as the taboo of virginity, a type of narcissistic character, the altruistic surrender, and a transitional object. These concepts, at a much later date, drew the attention of Freud, Anna Freud, and Winnicott.

Freud, in his 1922 letter, differentiated between the explorer and the artist in Schnitzler. Inevitably an analysis will always bring out the explorer and not the artistic ability. Nevertheless, we unwittingly seem to accept the idea that in the artist these two functions must be intimately connected. My critic friend stressed this point quite openly. Our evaluation of the artist depends upon how well he succeeds in developing a psychological understanding.

It is clear that such an understanding can be based only upon the artist's insight into his own development. This point leads to the question whether Schnitzler reveals any conflict of his own in this story. There is no material available to enable us to come to a conclusive answer.

[[RunningHead]]

Guides on Professional Conduct for Psychoanalysts

Joseph Michaels M.D.

Joseph Michaels

Joseph Michaels  

SUMMARY

Since 1941, the American Psychoanalytic Association has been engaged on and off in attempts to estabilsh a Code of Ethics. Various Committees ranging from professional conduct for the individual analyst to "unauthorized training" in relation to approved Institutes and fees to physicians have existed from time to time.

Edward Bibring, in 1951, provided the stimulus for a study pertaining to the establishment of a Code of Ethics for the Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, Inc. The philosophy and

308

methodology of the Committee on a Code of Ethics inaugurated by E. Bibring are discussed.

The final recommendations took the form of a Guide on Professional Conduct for Psychoanalysts and covered the following points:

1. The analyst in relation to his patients.

2. The training analyst in relation to the institute.

3. The training analyst in relation to the candidate.

4. The analyst in relation to the psychoanalytic institute.

5. The analyst in relation to the community.

A quarter of a century has elapsed since the sporadic attempts to establish a Code of Ethics for psychoanalysts were begun. It is time that they were completed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Symptom-Context Method—Quantitative Studies of Symptom Formation in Psychotherapy

Lester Luborsky Ph.D. and Arthur Auerbach M.D.

Arthur Auerbach

Lester Luborsky, Ph.D.
Arthur H. Auerbach, M.D.
207 Piersol Building, University Hospital University of Pennsylvania
Philadelphia, Pa. 19104

Arthur Auerbach  

SUMMARY

A systematic method of clinical observation is offered for research into the conditions surrounding the creation of memory, psychosomatic, and other symptoms. The symptom-context method is a way of collecting and analyzing symptoms which form during psychotherapy sessions and comparing these with control (nonsymptom) sessions. Psychotherapy and psychoanalysis offer an almost unique situation in which an observer (preferably aided by a tape recorder) is presented with a sufficient number of times on the rare occasions when a symptom emerges under one's eyes, and while the patient is revealing much of what he is experiencing.

Two conditions were found necessary for the onset of a symptom: (a) a particular type of immediate thought; (b) a larger background

97

psychobiological state of the organism. Both conditions working together are necessary.

Examples of three types of symptoms were described and analyzed: momentary forgetting, stomach pain, and migraine headache. The qualities which were most frequently found in the patients' speech just before the somatic symptoms were often related to the feeling of helplessness: concern about obtaining supplies, increased anxiety, loss of control, feeling blocked and frustrated by the therapist, and an increase in hopelessness. These qualities have been stressed by a number of writers as being crucial to the psychic state before the onset of symptoms, e.g., Freud (8), Engel and Schmale (6). Direct reference to the therapist appeared for both momentary forgetting and headache patients. The special values and assumptions of the method are listed.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Repetition Compulsion, Acting Out, and Identification with the Doer

Nathan Segel M.D.

Nathan Segel

Nathan P. Segel, M.D.
17535 Warrington Drive
Detroit, Mich. 48221

Nathan Segel  

SUMMARY

A clinical syndrome of exposure to early massive stimulation via primal scene involvement or the threat of abandonment is described as frequently being associated with acting out. This acting out, in turn, is seen as a later repetitive defensive effort to master or bind the anxiety by repeating in an active role aspects of the stimulating experiences that were passively imposed originally. This defensive ego activity, tentatively called an identification with the doer, uses the repetition compulsion to help achieve mastery over stimuli originating from within or without and is in keeping with the findings of Freud, Hartmann, and Waelder.

Clinical material is cited to illustrate these points. It is stressed that this identification with the doer could be used as a specific transference resistance to being flooded by stimuli from within in the process of recall, and hence must be handled by making repeated interpretations of its origin and genesis.

Finally, the identification with the doer is compared and contrasted with Anna Freud's conception of the identification with the aggressor. The latter is seen as a more circumscribed or limited concept where the defense is not only against the person of the aggressor, but is specifically directed against the feared aggressive impulses. In the case of identification with the doer, one is dealing with a more general defensive activity called up by the ego to defend equally against a flood of libidinal or aggressive impulses

486

regardless of whether the object from whom they originally emanated was involved in an active or passive role.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Problems of Termination in Child Analysis

Robert Kohrman M.D.

Robert Kohrman

Robert Kohrman  

In his opening remarks, Albert J. Solnit, described the unique idea around which this and a similar panel regarding adult analysis were organized. Samuel Guttman and Peter Neubauer had proposed that an ongoing dialogue with various senior analysts be instituted whereby they could present their experiences as regards the termination of an analysis, the criteria which guide them, and the various related issues and questions they find pertinent. The senior analysts were to be interviewed; statements of their opinions were to be presented (in their presence if possible) at a meeting and thus provide a springboard for discussion and reflection by all present. It was contemplated that this might be the first of a series of panels on the same topic or other topics using the same format. Not only would immediate purposes be served but a historically significant reservoir of knowledge might thus be collected. Solnit then further pointed out how child and adult analysis enrich and refine each other in a continuing manner. Child analysis contributes to the conceptual richness of adult analysis those developmental perspectives and formulations that emphasize and remind the psychoanalyst of the changing, adapting, and developing aspects of the adult personality, as well as the details, economy, and understanding of childhood reactions and attitudes. The analyst thus can be more specific and knowledgeable in his reconstruction of the childhood of his adult patients. Adult analysis, on the other hand, contributes to child analysis a clearer picture of the analytic situation, a dimension that enables the analyst to project and predict more usefully the future developmental alternatives for his child patients.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Boston, May 10, 1968. Chairman: Albert J. Solnit, M.D.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and Scientific Creativity—With Special Reference to Regression in the Service of the Ego
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Scientific Creativity—With Special Reference to Regression in the Service of the Ego

Marshall Bush

Marshall Bush

Marshall Bush
Mt. Zion Hospital Dept. of Psychiatry
1600 Divisadero Street
San Francisco, Calif. 94115

Marshall Bush  

SUMMARY

The first part of this paper deals with the research findings on the personality characteristics, intellectual style, and developmental experiences of researchers in the natural sciences. The motivational base of scientific interests is then discussed from the viewpoints of autonomous ego interests, drives and wishes, trauma and mastery, and various other personal issues which contribute to making science the arena in which an individual's most salient dynamics are lived out. The enduring aspects of a scientific identity and scientific interests are distinguished from the motivational complex underlying creative aspirations, as well as from the creative process itself, with the intent of isolating the multiple foci around which conflicts may enter into the scientific enterprise.

In the latter part of this paper, I have attempted to examine and clarify some of the psychoanalytic constructs frequently employed in explaining creative functioning from the viewpoint of their applicability to science. No conclusions are offered as to the essential nature of scientific creativity. Rather, it has been my aim to facilitate a broad multidimensional analysis of different types of scientific innovation. I believe that if someone were to undertake a careful psychological analysis of all the scientific innovations of one creative genius, such as Freud, the diversity of ways in which his contributions came about would be rather astonishing

186

and not lend themselves easily to any single formulation of a unitary "creative process."

One point of this paper has been to stress the need for distinguishing causal factors which lead to a creative result from a host of other psychological processes which merely accompany the creative act and codetermine many of its subjective qualities or else only indirectly influence the end product. Particular attention has been given here to the use of regression as an explanatory concept, and several conceptual distinctions have been suggested. It seems clear that regression, be it in the service of the ego or otherwise, is not a necessary or sufficient condition for scientific creation. It is often overemphasized and ambiguously employed, while the creative use of secondary-process functioning may be relatively neglected.

While the entire personality is involved in creative scientific work, it is not enough just to enumerate the personality variables which are pertinent for creative functioning. The type of analysis which is needed is an elucidation of how motives, conflicts, functions, and structures from all levels of the psychic hierarchy interact in scientific creation, i.e., which are direct causal determinants, which are facilitating factors, which are obstructive to creative work, and which are primarily ancillary. This type of analysis is needed for the individual creator as well as for various types of innovation before an adequate general psychoanalytic theory of scientific creativity can be formulated.

[[RunningHead]]

King Lear and the Use of Humor in Treatment

Gilbert Rose M.D.

Gilbert Rose

Gilbert J. Rose, M.D.
135 Rowayton Ave.
Rowayton, Conn. 06853

Gilbert Rose  

SUMMARY

The relationship of King Lear and his Fool might be viewed as an analytic therapy in which humor is an essential vehicle; the Fool confronts the King with the reality that he would deny; he interprets his castration anxiety and its derivatives; he attempts to support and protect his reality testing. In two crucial encounters distinct progress is made, but in the third the King's well-meaning friend intervenes and the battle for Lear's sanity is lost.

Given the therapist's talent for it, exercised with judgment, and operating from a stable therapeutic alliance to support an observant ego and highlight resistances, humor may act beneficially in treatment in several ways. It may be used to mobilize benign aspects of the superego, attack archaic elements of the superego, and support the ego. It may help lift repressions by making use of the mechanism of negation, and by fostering a favorable ambiance between the observing part of the patient's ego and the analyst; this may be internalized in the form of a freer interaction between the patient's unconscious and preconscious. Finally, humor may be useful in transmitting reality across ego boundaries in the right blend of distance and closeness.

responseSet.title: Some Early Manifestations of Transference—Their Implications for the First Phase of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Early Manifestations of Transference—Their Implications for the First Phase of Psychoanalysis

Robert Daniels M.D.

Robert Daniels

Robert S. Daniels, M.D.
University of Chicago Department of Psychiatry
950 East 59 Street
Chicago, Ill. 60637

Robert Daniels  

SUMMARY

1. Patients' responses to beginning analysis are stimulated by reactions to the psychoanalyst and the psychoanalytic process, a relationship and a situation uniquely structured to foster regression and observation. In particular, the total situation encourages the development of transferences and a repetition of the infantile neurosis in current form, the transference neurosis. Reactions to these processes take the form of defenses against the transference and the defense transferences. During the first phase of an analysis, the patient develops an alliance in which the observing ego, relatively conflict-free, operating mainly with neutralized energy, begins to perceive the work through these defensive maneuvers. This observing ego receives reinforcement from the positive transferences, the identifications, and the real situation.

2. This alliance has been variously termed a working alliance or a therapeutic alliance. This alliance is fostered by preliminary clarifying, educative, and interpretative comments which, although they are incomplete interpretations, offer preliminary demonstrations of the method and its value. These demonstrations initiate the process of acquiring insight and encourage the patient's

collaboration in a method which he may actively employ. The case material cited supports Gitelson's (1962) contention that the patient-analyst diatrophic relationship is an important determinant in the patient's capacity to establish an analytic alliance. The capacity for the patient to participate in a diatrophic relationship with his psychoanalyst is based on his original dyadic relationship with his mother during the first two years of life. The more severe the trauma occurring during that time, the more difficult is the establishment and maintenance of the alliance.

3. The case material is not helpful in answering technical questions about the effect of early deep transference interpretations. In these cases defense was interpreted before content in an attempt to understand and undermine ego defense. Whether early deep transference interpretations might have reduced anxiety in Cases 2 and 3 and contributed to quicker and more effective progress through the first phase remains uncertain. Current differences in the technical approach to the first phase warrant careful experimentation to compare and contrast the consequences of such differences for the first and subsequent phases of analysis.

4. Defense against the transference and the defense transference are helpful concepts in ordering and understanding the phenomena of the first phase of analysis. For example, in Case 1, possible induction into military service became a potent defense against the transference which markedly slowed progress in the first phase. All three cases demonstrate common defense transferences. As recognized by analyst and patient they provide valuable insights into the nature of the conflict, the type and extent of the anxiety, the typical character manifestations, other ego and superego functions, and fixation and regression points. They offer the possibility for beginning insight into aspects of the later transference neurosis and are predictors about the likelihood of success in the analysis as well as the nature and kind of result. Particularly severe and unyielding defenses against the transference and defense transferences may interfere with the treatment. Defense transference countertransference fits between patient and analyst may also lead to interminable analyses with reinforcement of character patterns rather than change. A possible example of such a stalemate was discussed in Case 3.

Unexpected Death in a Patient who Wished to Die

John Coolidge M.D.

John Coolidge

John C. Coolidge, M.D.
141 Coolidge Hill
Cambridge, Mass. 02138

John Coolidge  

SUMMARY

The literature regarding spontaneous death from unknown causes at the time of severe emotional stress is briefly reviewed. In each instance hopelessness and a deep conviction that death would ensue was present. The cited case presented material from the analysis of a middle-aged woman who felt hopelessly trapped in an impossible life situation. Accompanying a growing conviction that

419

death would be the only solution was such intense desperation and panic that physiological processes were increasingly disrupted, finally leading to spontaneous ventricular fibrillation and eventual death. Evidence suggests that she was actively taught as a child by her rejecting parents to block all external channels of emotional expression. Her lifelong history of manifold physiological disturbance at times of stress gives evidence to the belief that the only route psychologically available for discharge of emotional tensions was internal and somatic. It is postulated that massive internal discharge overwhelmed the regulating mechanisms of the autonomic nervous system to the extent that vital functions could no longer be sustained.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Iceman Changeth—Toward an Empirical Approach to Creativity

Albert Rothenberg M.D.

Albert Rothenberg

Albert Rothenberg, M.D.
Yale University School of Medicine
333 Cedar Street
New Haven, Conn. 06510

Albert Rothenberg  

SUMMARY

It is extraordinarily difficult to develop an adequate empirical methodology for the study of creativity. In the light of the methodological and theoretical pitfalls discussed in the present paper, it would seem that the most reliable empirical approach to creativity is to begin with a focus on the creative process in the arts, i.e., an attempt to study an artist while in the process of creating.

A broad ongoing project of interviewing living writers with

591

a focus on work in progress has been undertaken along with control studies of revisions in the manuscripts of dead writers. Revisions in the manuscripts of Eugene O'Neill's play, The Iceman Cometh, were studied intensively and findings were correlated with life history material obtained from Mrs. Carlotta O'Neill and other sources.

Revision patterns around the central symbol of the iceman indicate that O'Neill was preoccupied with infidelity at the time he was writing the play. Viewed in relation to other information about the life history sources of the major themes in the play, this finding suggests some hypotheses about kinds of psychological processes O'Neill utilized in the creation of the iceman symbol. These processes may be generally involved in artistic creation:

1. Artistic symbols contain a concrete and abstract aspect at the same time, but the development of symbols starts from the concrete and proceeds to the abstract aspect.

2. The artist separates himself from his creation by a process of deleting out material which is closest to his own immediate preconscious wishes and motivations.

Other hypotheses about O'Neill's psychological processes in the creation of the iceman symbol pertain to the creative process in areas other than art as well:

1. The development of ideas in creation proceeds in part by a process of oppositional thinking, an ego process in which a notion and its contradiction or opposite are conceptualized simultaneously. This occurs on the secondary-process level primarily, but can account for the appearance of primary-process elements in creation.

2. The creator integrates contradictory or oppositional ideas which have emotional importance to him personally as well as to society at large. He chooses which of these integrations to utilize in his creations on the basis of his knowledge of his field as well as the emotional importance to him.

3. The creator chooses which ideas have value on the basis of their economy. This can account for the fact that creative ideas in both art and science have aesthetic appeal.
